content.text: War Room Tooling and Collaboration Standards

Every tool in the war room exists to serve a specific coordination function. A Grafana dashboard that no one knows how to navigate is not a monitoring tool during an incident -- it is a distraction. This document defines which tools are required at each severity level, how they integrate with the IC bot, and the collaboration standards that govern how information moves between them.

The guiding principle is single source of truth: each category of information (alerts, task queue, timeline, external status) lives in exactly one place. Information that exists in two places will diverge. When it diverges during a P0, the IC is forced to spend cognitive bandwidth reconciling it instead of driving resolution.

attrs.alt: Tool integration stack diagram: IC Bot at the center connects to PagerDuty (alerting), Zoom (bridge call), Grafana (dashboards), Notion (postmortem), and Status Page (customer updates). All integrations are bidirectional.

content.text: Not every tool needs to be active for every incident. Running the full stack for a P3 creates overhead without value. The table below defines the minimum required toolset at each severity.

The IC bot is a Slack app that integrates with PagerDuty, Zoom, Grafana, Notion, and the status page. It exposes a command interface and also receives webhook events from each tool. Understanding the bidirectional event model prevents the IC from manually replicating state that the bot already tracks.
Creates channels, pages oncall, starts postmortem draft
Channel links, PD incident ID, Notion URL
Adds user to bridge and tech channels, records assignment
Adds user to bridge and comms channels
Tags user as Scribe; bot will prompt them to log timeline entries
Starts Zoom meeting, pins link, begins recording
Captures current Grafana incident dashboard, attaches to postmortem
Updates severity, re-routes PD, updates channel topic
Severity change event to all channels
Adds to the task queue shown in bridge channel
Records resolution, archives channels, converts postmortem
The IC bot subscribes to events from each integrated tool. When an event arrives, the bot posts a structured notification to the bridge channel so the IC does not need to watch multiple dashboards.

Each Claude service has a standard incident dashboard pre-configured in Grafana. The IC bot links this dashboard in the bridge channel at declaration. The Tech Lead is responsible for annotating the dashboard at key events -- mitigation deployed, traffic drained, rollback initiated -- so the timeline on the dashboard matches the incident timeline in Notion.

import requests

GRAFANA_API_URL = "https://grafana.anthropic.com/api/annotations"

def annotate_incident_event(
    api_key: str,
    dashboard_uid: str,
    incident_id: str,
    event_text: str,
    tags: list[str],
) -> int:
    """
    Post a timestamped annotation to the Grafana incident dashboard.
    Returns the annotation ID.
    """
    response = requests.post(
        GRAFANA_API_URL,
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"},
        json={
            "dashboardUID": dashboard_uid,
            "time": int(__import__("time").time() * 1000),  # milliseconds
            "tags": [f"incident:{incident_id}"] + tags,
            "text": event_text,
        },
        timeout=5,
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()["id"]

# Usage:
# annotate_incident_event(
#     api_key=os.environ["GRAFANA_API_KEY"],
#     dashboard_uid="claude-api-overview",
#     incident_id="INC-20240719-004",
#     event_text="Rollback to v4.2.1 initiated by @sre-oncall",
#     tags=["mitigation", "rollback"],
# )
Every Claude API incident dashboard includes the following panels. Teams must not modify or remove these panels during an incident; dashboard changes go through a change review outside incident time.
Primary signal. Shown prominently at top left. Red threshold line at 1%; orange at 5%; red fill above 25%.
Three lines on a single panel. SLA threshold overlaid as a dashed line.
Separate lines per request type. Baseline and -5% threshold marked.
Stacked area chart. Used to confirm traffic drain is complete.
Red/green indicator per region. Flips on /health endpoint failure.
Shows all IC bot annotations as vertical markers.

The postmortem draft is created by the IC bot at incident declaration. It pre-populates the incident ID, severity, declared time, and links to the bridge channel. The Scribe is responsible for keeping the timeline section current throughout the incident; the IC writes the root cause and action items sections after resolution.
After resolution, reviewed in postmortem meeting
, the bot timestamps the entry and appends it to both the Notion timeline and the bridge channel. This means the Scribe can log events without leaving Slack.

# Scribe commands:
/ic log "Traffic drain to eu-west-1 started by @sre-oncall"
/ic log "Quality score drop confirmed: -18% on claude-3-5-haiku us-east-1 only"
/ic log "Model version rollback to v4.2.1 initiated"
/ic log "Quality score recovering: now -6% below baseline"
/ic log "All metrics nominal for 30 minutes -- mitigation confirmed"

# Bot appends each entry to Notion timeline with UTC timestamp
# and posts a brief confirmation in the bridge channel.

The hypothesis board is a shared, structured investigation space. It prevents two engineers from investigating the same hypothesis independently and prevents the team from forgetting a hypothesis that was raised but not yet investigated.
The hypothesis board has three columns:
Hypotheses with evidence. Root cause candidates that have been tested and verified.
Active investigations. Each entry must have an owner. Maximum two active investigations per engineer.
Hypotheses that have been eliminated with evidence. Must include a brief note on why they were ruled out.
For remote incidents the board lives in a pinned Notion page linked from the tech channel. For physical war rooms it is a whiteboard photographed every 30 minutes.

<!-- Format: [H-{number}] {hypothesis} ({owner}) -->

## CONFIRMED
- [H-03] Quality drop is model-serving only, not pre-processing. Confirmed via A/B
         comparison of request logs before and after serving gateway. (@model-eng)

## INVESTIGATING
- [H-07] Memory pressure on serving fleet causing quality degradation. Checking
         memory metrics in us-east-1. (@sre-oncall) -- started 14:32

## RULED OUT
- [H-01] Config change in last 30 min. Ruled out: no config deploys in past 4h.
         Config diff attached.
- [H-02] Client-side issue / SDK bug. Ruled out: internal API test shows same degradation.
- [H-04] eu-west-1 also affected. Ruled out: eu-west-1 quality metrics nominal.
- [H-05] Network routing issue. Ruled out: request latency normal, error 4xx not 5xx.
- [H-06] Upstream dependency (tokeniser). Ruled out: tokeniser logs clean.
Tool overuse and misuse are as damaging as tool absence. The following patterns recur across incidents and should be explicitly called out in postmortems when they occur.
Engineers stare at dashboards waiting for a signal instead of forming and testing hypotheses
Assign hypothesis owners; dashboards confirm/refute hypotheses, they do not generate them
Multiple engineers editing the timeline simultaneously create merge conflicts and lost entries
Only the Scribe edits the timeline; others use /ic log commands
Engineers share long log excerpts verbally; information not recorded
Findings go in #inc-{id}-tech as Slack messages or code blocks; call used for decisions only
Ten open hypotheses with no owners; no one knows what is being investigated
Max two active hypotheses per engineer; Ruled Out column must be maintained
Multiple on-calls each create a PD incident for the same event
IC bot links all alerts to the single PD incident at declaration
Updates every 5 min with minor wording changes erode customer trust
Post at state transitions (Investigating -> Identified -> Monitoring -> Resolved) only

The IC bot maintains a shared task queue displayed as a pinned message in the bridge channel. Every in-flight action that has been explicitly delegated appears in this queue. The IC uses the queue to track what is in progress without having to ask for status updates in the bridge.
The pinned task queue updates in real time as the IC and team members run task commands:

## Active Tasks -- INC-20240719-004 (updated 14:47)

| ID   | Owner        | Action                               | Status      | ETA   |
|------|--------------|--------------------------------------|-------------|-------|
| T-01 | @sre-oncall  | Drain traffic from us-east-1         | in-progress | 14:50 |
| T-02 | @model-eng   | Pull serving logs for quality drop   | in-progress | -     |
| T-03 | @tech-lead   | Confirm eu-west-1 unaffected         | done        | -     |
| T-04 | @sre-2       | Stand up shadow canary in us-west-2  | pending     | -     |

Completed: T-03 (14:38) | Blocked: none

# Add a task (IC or Tech Lead):
/ic task add --owner @sre-oncall --action "Drain traffic from us-east-1" --eta 14:50

# Mark in progress (assignee):
/ic task start T-01

# Mark done (assignee or IC):
/ic task done T-01

# Block a task (assignee):
/ic task block T-04 --reason "Canary environment quota exhausted"

# IC views summary in bridge channel at any time:
/ic task list

When a task is blocked, the bot posts a notification to the bridge channel immediately. A blocked task is the IC's signal to either unblock it (reallocate resources, escalate the blocker) or reassign it to an unblocked path.
Tool Runbook: What To Do When a Tool Fails
War room tools can fail during an incident. Each tool has a defined fallback that the IC uses without discussion -- the fallback is not a decision to be made under pressure.
IC posts in existing channel if visible; switches to PD conference bridge if not
IC calls oncall engineer directly from the on-call rotation contact sheet
Google Meet (backup link pre-provisioned per incident)
Bot posts backup link at bridge-call creation time
Link to fallback in #inc-runbook pinned message
Shared Google Doc (IC creates from template)
IC runs /ic fallback-doc; bot creates and shares Google Doc
Post to @AnthropicStatus Twitter/X directly via Social team
Comms Lead contacts Social team; IC approves tweet text

When one or more tools are unavailable, the IC declares degraded mode in the bridge channel. This signals to all participants that they should expect slower information flow and that fallback procedures are active.

# IC posts in bridge channel when a tool is unavailable:
/ic degraded-mode --tool zoom --fallback "Google Meet: meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij"

# Bot posts a pinned banner in the bridge channel:
# :warning: DEGRADED MODE: Zoom unavailable.
# Fallback bridge: meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij
# All other tools nominal.
War room tools are themselves services with availability targets. These SLOs are tracked separately from product SLOs and are the responsibility of the Infrastructure team, not the incident team.

The status page is hosted on a separate infrastructure stack from the Claude API specifically so that a Claude outage does not prevent communication about the Claude outage. This is a hard architectural requirement, not an SLO suggestion.

The war room tool stack integrates Slack (IC bot), PagerDuty, Zoom, Grafana, Notion, and the status page into a single coordinated environment. The IC bot is the central orchestrator: it provisions channels, pages on-call, opens bridge calls, captures dashboard snapshots, maintains the task queue, and routes webhook events from each tool back to the bridge channel. Each tool has a single function: PagerDuty for alerting, Zoom for verbal coordination, Grafana for metrics, Notion for the postmortem and timeline, and the status page for customer communication. The hypothesis board -- either a pinned Notion page or a physical whiteboard -- provides a structured investigation space with confirmed, investigating, and ruled-out columns. Tool anti-patterns (dashboard paralysis, parallel Notion editing, hypothesis sprawl) are identified in postmortems and addressed through process updates and IC training.
